hospice.description: The leaders of the hospice and palliative care service are formally or informally identified.
The hospice and palliative care service have a mission statement that reflects its strategic objectives and matches the needs of the community served.
The leaders are collectively responsible for ensuring that the mission statement is known to all personnel, patients, carers and the community served.
The hospice and palliative care service participate in community health activities.
The leaders, in liaison with the whole hospice and palliative care service team, coordinates the compilation of a strategic plan and an annual budget.
The leaders are collectively responsible for implementing the service's mission and strategic plan.
The strategic plan is evaluated on an annual basis.
Regular monitoring of the implementation of the strategic plan against envisaged timeframes and progress in achieving its objectives is documented at intervals determined by the service.
Where appropriate, the leadership roles in various positions are documented, agreed to and known by the personnel.
The manager is responsible for the day-to-day running ofthe service.
The manager has the education and/or experience necessary to carry out his or her responsibilities.
The hospice and palliative care service is licensed in terms of relevant legislation for the level and type of services provided.
The manager ensures that there is a system in place to monitor the implementation of applicable laws and regulations.
There is evidence of response to any reports from inspecting and regulatory bodies/organisations
The manager implements processes to manage and control human, financial and other resources.
The hospice and palliative care service leaders facilitate communication between teams where relevant and between individual personnel members.
Agendas for meetings are prepared and shared in order to allow those attending to prepare for participation.
Minutes of meetings are taken and circulated to all relevant personnel.
There is a procedure to make sure that important matters resulting from management meetings are communicated to and acted upon by personnel.

The hospice and palliative care service plans and coordinates comprehensive care by establishing relations and maintaining contact with other relevant services and agencies, including both governmental and non- governmental agencies.
Information on services, hours of operation and processes for obtaining care is provided to services in the community who work in collaboration with the hospice and palliative care service.
The hospice and palliative care service determines that the receiving individual/organisation can meet the patient's continuing care needs and establishes arrangements to ensure continuity.
Policies and procedures that guide and support the different services offered by the hospice and palliative care service are implemented.
Policies and procedures are signed and dated by persons authorised to do so.
Policies and procedures are correctly compiled, indexed and filed.
Policies and procedures are reviewed according to organisational policy and then dated and signed.
There is a process to ensure that personnel are familiar with relevant policies and procedures.
There are documented processes for staffing the service.
The desired education, qualifications, skills and knowledge are defined for personnel.
Personnel members in the fields of spiritual care, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, dietetics and complementary therapies are available.
The processes include measures to improve personnel retention.
The process includes that health professional support and supervision is provided to community caregivers, other non-professional personnel and volunteers involved in patient care.
The process includes that the individuals responsible for patient care are identified by name badges.
There is implementation of a reliable, documented process for evaluating and verifying the credentials of all health professionals.
Personnel files contain copies of qualifications and licences/registration from the relevant authority for all health professionals.
There is a system to track the annual registration of all health professionals.
All personnel with direct contact with the public have had a police check, a copy of which is kept in their personnel file.
There are documented processes for orientation of personnel to the hospice and palliative care service.
The hospice and palliative care service support continuing education for its clinical personnel and maintains records of this in personnel files.
There is a development strategy for the service that ensures that the service manager and administrative personnel receive the training required to fulfil their responsibilities.
Personnel members are informed of opportunities to participate in advanced education, training, research, and other experiences.
An individual is designated to coordinate the volunteer service.
The volunteer coordinator attends management meetings or is part of the management team.
The services provided by volunteers are documented in a signed agreement with the organisation.
Volunteers are clearly identified by name badges.
A designated person is responsible for the implementation and maintenance of a financial strategy.
The designated person is suitably qualified and/or experienced in accounting and financial management.
The responsibilities of this person include ensuring that policies and procedures for all financial functions are implemented.
There is a current budget for the hospice and palliative care service.
A report is produced at least annually, setting out the financial position to date.
There is a mechanism for establishing the reason for budget variation in either income or expenditure.
Capital investment proposals are subject to unanimous agreement among the partners or are agreed according to a voting system acceptable to all partners in the service.
The hospice and palliative care service have documented ethical and legal policies and procedures for the financial management of the service that are implemented.
Internal and external financial audit systems which meet audit requirements are maintained.
Annual audited financial statements are produced within the required time frame.
There is a capital asset register, which is routinely maintained.
Assets are insured.
All health professionals provide evidence of professional indemnity insurance.
An individual is designated to control the ordering, storage, distribution and control of equipment and supplies used in the service.
Policies and procedures relating to all aspects of provisioning/supply chain management are implemented.
Secure storage facilities are available.
Prescription pads, letterheads, investigation request forms, administrative records and other official documents are accessible only to authorised persons.

There are documented risk management processes for identifying all risks relating to hospice and palliative care processes and systems, personnel, patients, visitors to the service and physical facilities.
The service manager ensures the development and implementation of written policies and procedures for risk management processes and activities.
A nominated individual with relevant qualifications, skills and/or experience supervises the implementation of the risk management programme.
Ongoing in-service training of all personnel in risk management policies, procedures and principles is documented.
Risk management systems are reviewed whenever there are changes in systems and processes or physical facilities.
A nominated individual is responsible for infection control in the service and personnel are aware of the nomination.
Written policies and procedures guide personnel in the implementation of the infection control programme.
Regular in-service training is given to all personnel in the field of infection control and is documented.
Infection control is on the agenda of all personnel meetings and discussion points are documented.
All patient and personnel areas of the service are included in the documented infection control programme.
Hand washing and disinfecting facilities, including warm water, soap, paper towels or hand sanitisers are available in all relevant areas.
Personnel are constantly reminded of the importance of effective hand washing.
The service reports on notifiable diseases to appropriate external public health agencies.
A nominated individual has been trained in and is responsible for sterilisation procedures within the service and can describe the process in detail.
Relevant personnel members are immunised against Hepatitis B according to the policy.
There is a documented policy for the management of exposure to high risk infections and needle-stick injuries.
Post exposure prophylaxis is available to personnel in accordance with national policy and includes the management of the patient from whom the needle was withdrawn.
There is a documented policy for the management of body fluid spills.
There is a documented policy for the triage of patients with potential communicable diseases.

The hospice and palliative care service have a waste management policy that is consistent with country- specific laws and regulations and includes the safe handling, storing and disposing of all different types of waste.
The policy makes provision for the appropriate management of confidential waste.
Waste is segregated in accordance with policies, procedures, country-specific laws and regulations.
The colour of bag and type of container appropriate to the type of waste generated are available.
Waste is protected from theft, vandalism or scavenging by animals.
Waste is collected at appropriate times so that hazards are not caused.
Security systems, including guards if required, provide for internal and external security.
Sufficient light sources are available to provide adequate light (no dark areas) in all areas such as the entrance, waiting rooms, halls and offices.
There is effective control of access to clinical areas and store areas.
Alarm systems and signals are tested every month.
A mechanism known to the personnel is available for summoning the assistance of security/police/protection services in the case of an emergency.
Reasonable measures are taken to ensure the safety of lone workers.
There are structured systems and processes in place to ensure that all occupants of the service's facilities are safe from fire or smoke.
Documented certification is available from the relevant authority to show that the facility complies with applicable laws and regulations in relation to fire safety (Fire clearance certificate).
Firefighting equipment is regularly inspected and serviced at least annually, and the date of the service is recorded on the apparatus.
Flammable materials are clearly labelled and safely stored.
Sufficient electrical socket outlets are provided in all areas to avoid overloading of individual outlets and to minimise fire risks.
Easily recognised and understood signs prohibiting smoking are displayed in areas where flammable materials and combustible gases are stored.
A floor plan showing the location of firefighting equipment, electrical distribution board, evacuation routes and emergency exits is displayed.
Annual personnel training in fire prevention and evacuation procedures is documented.
A mechanism known to personnel is available for summoning the fire service.
There is a written plan to deal with emergencies (including bomb threats, flooding, natural disasters, failure of water and electrical supplies).
There are site and floor plans that depict the locations and layout of the main services (for example, water, sanitation, electricity supply).
Documented evidence is available to show that the personnel participate in a rehearsal of the emergency plan at least annually.
First aid kits and materials for healthcare workers are available.
There is a documented policy for collection, collation, validation and distribution of data which is implemented.
Clinical, managerial and administrative personnel participate in developing and implementing an information system to support patient care and service management.
Clinical and managerial data and information are integrated as needed to support decision-making.
Required technology and other resources support the implementation of the data and information management system.
Security and confidentiality of data and information is maintained.
Documented procedures which outline the processes to provide required information to individuals and agencies outside the service when required by laws or regulations are implemented.
The service manager or delegated person checks the integrity of data leaving the facility for completeness, correctness and consistency.
Data is aggregated, analysed and transformed into useful, relevant information for monitoring and improving the service.
The frequency of data collection and analysis is appropriate to the process under study.
Statistical tools and techniques are used in the analysis process when suitable.
Information relating to the quality of the services delivered, is made available to the patients of the hospice and palliative care service and other relevant parties.
A designated individual oversees the information and quality management and improvement processes.
All service personnel are informed about the function of the information and quality management individual.
There are formal systems and processes for quality management and improvement.
Personnel are trained in the implementation of quality management processes.

The service manager provides technology, support and resources (including time) for the assessment and improvement of the service's management, clinical and support processes and this is reflected in the strategic plan/business plan for the service.
The leaders set priorities for improvement activities based on high risk, high cost and/or high volume or problem-prone areas.
Each function within the service implements relevant quality improvement activities.
The objectives, scope, implementation and effectiveness of the activities to assess and improve quality are evaluated regularly and revised as necessary.
Targets (goals) are set for the desired levels of patient care and service management.
The service collects data relevant to each identified indicator in the quality improvement programme for the monitoring and improvement of patient care and service management.
As part of clinical monitoring, structured clinical audits are done to monitor the implementation of clinical guidelines.
Information from the findings of quality assessment and improvement activities is used to detect trends, patterns and opportunities to improve or prevent potential problems.
When appropriate, an improvement plan is developed in collaboration with all relevant team members and an implementation process and acceptable timeframe is agreed to by the team and implemented.
A time for repeat data collection and analysis is agreed and completed and the results discussed by the relevant team members.
The service holds regular meetings to discuss significant clinical issues.
Information from a validated patient/family satisfaction audit tool is used to improve the quality of service delivery.

A documented procedure for the monitoring of near misses/incidents/adverse (sentinel) events is available, which includes the documentation of interventions, responses and corrective actions taken to recorded incidents.
Formal significant event analyses are undertaken when necessary.
Notes are kept regarding the data analysis and actions arising from formal incident reviews.
All changes to policy and procedure that are identified as a result of these formal incident reviews are documented and included in new policies or incorporated into existing policies.
The implementation of these new policies/procedures is delegated to a nominated individual who is responsible for monitoring the effectiveness of the changes and arranging reviews if appropriate.
Patient records are reviewed regularly, and results analysed as part of the quality improvement process.
The review uses a representative sample.
Records comply with professionally acceptable norms (Including legal requirements where applicable) relating to signature, use of abbreviations and legibility.
Standardised diagnosis and procedure codes are used.
Symbols and definitions are standardised.
Directional signs to the service are clearly readable and up to date.
A telephone/emergency number is available and provided to patients on registration and on request.
A phone number for the after-hours service is clearly displayed on the outside of the facility.
Parking is provided close to the building entrance for patients, including the physically challenged.
There is wheelchair access to and within the building.
Ramps and stairs include safety features such as rails.
Laws, regulations and other requirements applicable to the organisation’s facilities are implemented and are available to the personnel.
The building is appropriate as a healthcare facility in terms of size and lay-out.
The lay-out of the facility allows for effective flow of patient care.
The waiting area is sufficient to accommodate the usual number of patients and other people who could be waiting at any given time.
The waiting area caters for the specific safety needs of children, where applicable.
There is a designated space that offers privacy for every member of the interdisciplinary team working in the service at any time.
All areas of the facility, including consultation rooms are clean, well ventilated and well maintained.
Sufficient office/administrative space is available for the personnel.
Toilet/washroom facilities are clean and in working order.
Separate sanitary facilities are provided for personnel.
There is a separate, secure area for personnel with adequate secure storage facilities for outdoor clothing, handbags and personal possessions.
Required furniture is available according to established lists and functioning properly.
Hand washing facilities, including water, soap and paper towels are available for patients and personnel.
Implemented policies and procedures guide the management of medical equipment.
A designated individual supervises the management of medical equipment in the service.
There is an inventory of the medical equipment available in the service.
The supply of medical equipment is adequate to meet the needs of the service.
Records are kept of the checking and maintenance of medical equipment.
There is a documented procedure known to the personnel for reporting defects in medical equipment.
Written policies and procedures guide service personnel on the implementation of all maintenance service requirements.
A designated, competent individual is responsible for supervising the maintenance of buildings, grounds and utilities.
Where these services are outsourced the, personnel have access to a list of these private contractors/service providers with their contact numbers at all times.
Written agreements ensure technical back-up services are available at all times during the opening or operational hours of the service.
Basic maintenance equipment, tools and spare parts are available.
The service plans and budgets for the upgrading or replacing of systems, buildings or components needed for the continued operation of a safe and effective facility.
The service has a documented preventative maintenance management plan in place.
Documented inspections of the buildings and grounds are conducted at regular intervals as determined by policy.
There is a documented procedure known to the personnel for reporting defects.
Policies and procedures guide the management of ICT equipment are implemented.
A designated individual supervises the management of ICT equipment in the service.
There is an inventory of all ICT equipment.
All desktop and server computers are provided with surge protection and the server is protected by an uninterruptable power supply.
A documented policy is available clearly describing appropriate back up procedures for electronic records.
Regular checks are made and documented to ensure that backup has been successful.
Records are kept of the checking and maintenance of ICT equipment.
The service has appropriate and up to date virus protection software and firewall protection to ensure adequate security and confidentiality of patient related information.
There is documented evidence that relevant personnel are regularly trained to use/operate ICT equipment.
Policies and procedures for the storage, retrieval, retention, destruction and confidentiality and safety of patient records and personal information are implemented.
Designated individuals are responsible for the storage, maintenance and retrieval of patient files.
The filing system allows for incorrectly filed records to be easily identified (for example, through colour coding of the records).
Storage space for health records is sufficient and secure against unauthorised entry.
Records that have been removed from the designated storage area for use are kept out of public view.
The retention process provides the necessary confidentiality and security.
Written policies and procedures relating to cleaning and laundry duties and the frequency with which these duties are carried out are implemented and monitored.
There is evidence that laundry used for patients is washed separately from domestic laundry.
Adequate, secure and well-ventilated storage areas are available for cleaning and laundry equipment and chemicals.
Mops and brooms are cleaned and dried before being stored.

The service manager ensures that cleaning and laundry personnel are appropriately trained regarding waste management, infection control procedures, confidentiality issues and any other relevant matters.
A person who is suitably qualified or experienced manages/supervises the service.
The responsibilities of the manager/supervisor are documented.
The manager/supervisor ensures that policies and procedures that address at least items (a) to (g) in the intent statement are implemented.
Where a kitchen is provided within the facility, it is designed to allow for the effective storage, preparation and serving of food.
There is a section of the kitchen dedicated to the preparation of infant feeds, where necessary.
There are separate hand-washing facilities in the food preparation area, with soap and paper towels.
There is a mechanism to prevent unauthorised individuals from entering food preparation areas.
The temperature, ventilation and humidity levels are adequate to provide for satisfactory working conditions and cleanliness.
Windows in the preparation area have fly screens or another effective method of fly control is available.
There is a fire extinguisher and a fire blanket in the kitchen.
The food service area meets with health and safety regulations.
Food handlers wear protective clothing.
Protective wear is provided for visitors entering the food preparation area.
Equipment, floors, walls and ceilings are kept clean.
Personnel are constantly reminded of the importance of effective hand washing.
Preparation surfaces are cleaned and dried between being used for different activities.
There are adequate clean and conveniently placed change rooms, toilets and ablution facilities for food handlers.
Food handlers have access to lockers for their outer clothing.
An adequate number of suitable refuse containers are provided in or near each change room, hand washing facility and toilet area.
A suitably qualified and/or experienced person advises on meal planning.
There is a planned weekly menu suitable for different seasons.
Wherever possible, patient food preferences are respected, and substitutions made available.
Food which is of a potentially high risk, unprepared and prepared food, are kept separately.
Separate cutting boards are kept for raw and cooked food.
Food is kept for a minimal amount of time after cooking and before serving.
Food waste is put in covered containers and removed without delay from places where food is prepared.

There is a system in place to ensure that food handlers report conditions that may result in the transmission of infection to other food handlers or to individuals consuming food prepared in the kitchen and appropriate action is taken to prevent transmission.
Food is stored at acceptable temperatures.
Food is stored separately from non-food items.
Different types of food are kept separately.
Food is stored off the ground on racks or shelving of an impenetrable material.
Walk-in refrigerators and freezers are capable of being opened from inside through a safety release mechanism.
Stock is rotated.
Copies of contracts for outside service providers are available to those who ensure they are implemented.
Services provided under contracts and other arrangements are formally monitored and compliance with the contract is documented.
Contracted personnel are managed as determined in the written service agreement.
The service manager ensures that contracted personnel are oriented to the service and to relevant policies and procedures.
The service manager ensures that contracted personnel participate in relevant in-service training programmes.
The interdisciplinary team uses current guidelines relevant to the hospice and palliative care service, to assist in the management of patients.
The interdisciplinary team set criteria to select hospice and palliative care guidelines.
The interdisciplinary team can describe how they ensure consistency in the management of patients.
Each patient has an initial assessment that complies with current policies, procedures and guidelines.
A paediatric assessment form is used for the assessment of children.
The scope and content of the initial assessment is defined for each member of the multidisciplinary team.
Only those individuals permitted by applicable legislation perform the assessments.
The findings of assessments performed outside the organisation are indicated in a patient referral letter.
Initial assessments are completed within a time frame established by the organisation.
All patients are reassessed at defined intervals, to determine their response to treatment and to plan for continued care.
All assessment findings, including initial and reassessments are documented in the patient health record.
Patients assessed as being at nutritional risk receive nutrition therapy from a suitably qualified person.
Based on information obtained during the admission process, a holistic plan of care is developed, recorded and implemented for each patient.
The care plan is compiled based on the problems, needs and strengths identified in the initial assessment.
Where appropriate, the plan of care indicates a time frame for the expected achievement of results.
The care plan indicates the expected outcomes of planned interventions.
Policies guide the frequency with which plans of care must be reviewed, and each discipline or service reviews and revises as necessary those aspects of the care plan for which they are responsible.
Patients are referred for further investigation and/or treatment as appropriate.
Patients identified as being at risk of tuberculosis are referred for diagnosis and treatment.
The assessment process makes provision for patients in pain to be identified.
Patients in pain receive care according to pain and symptom management guidelines.
There is evidence that the effectiveness of pain and symptom management is monitored.
The organisation has processes to educate health professionals in assessing and managing pain and symptoms.
There is evidence that patients and families are educated about pain and symptom management.
Policies and procedures that guide the care of dying patients are implemented.
The unique needs of dying patients are recognised and respected.
There is evidence that the care plan is revised to meet the needs of the patient and family during this phase of the illness.
Personnel provide respectful and compassionate care to dying patients and their families.
Post-death care is delivered in a respectful manner, taking cultural and religious practices into account.
Referral and access to bereavement support is made when indicated.
The organisation has a system for providing support for personnel and volunteers.

When patients register with the hospice and palliative care service they are informed of the range of services offered by the service, opening hours, contact details and after hours’ care arrangements.
Where the service has an 'on hold' telephone message, it includes a message for an alternative number to be used in an emergency.
A message on the service’s telephone answering machine, call diversion system or paging system provide information to patients on how to obtain care outside the service’s normal opening hours.
The service renders services based on the needs of the population, during the hours published.
Patients (and their families or carers as appropriate) are provided with information on the estimated cost of services to be rendered.
In the case of admission to an in-patient unit, patients (and their families or carers as appropriate) are provided with information on any limitation placed on the duration of stay.
There is a documented process to refer patients.
The lines of communication between the hospice and palliative care service, referral hospital and community services are clearly defined.
The doctor informs the patient if he/she has any financial interest in the referral service.
Referrals are to specific individuals and/or agencies in the patient's home community wherever possible.
Patients and as appropriate their families are given follow-up instructions which are provided in an understandable form and manner.
A copy of the referral note is available in the patient record.
The referral is made on appropriate stationery or electronically.
The referral contains as a minimum a) – g) in the standard intent above.
The hospice and palliative care service keep a record of replies received following the referral of patients.
There are written guidelines for the referral of emergency patients.
Follow-up care based on the findings of investigations/consultations performed outside the hospice and palliative care service are noted in the patient record.
New patients to the service are asked to complete a form detailing at least a) – o) in the standard intent above.
Patients are asked to update the organisation if their contact details change.
The service implements a policy on telephonic and electronic communication with patients that details at least a) – e) in the standard intent above.
There is evidence of service/patient telephone or electronic advice and information in the patient health records.
The service can demonstrate how it receives and returns telephone and electronic messages from patients.
A documented policy on the review and management of pathology results, imaging reports, investigation reports and other clinical correspondence received by the palliative care service is implemented.

Patient health records contain evidence that all pathology results, imaging reports, investigation reports and clinical correspondence received by the service have been handled in accordance with policy.
There is evidence of review of the results of procedures and diagnostic tests performed by a medical practitioner.
There is a system to advise patients of the process to follow up on results.
A documented system to identify, follow up and recall patients with clinically significant results is implemented.
When patients are informed of results over the telephone, the patient is identified with 3 approved identifiers before the information is given.
When results are given over the telephone, the result is given to the patient themselves or the patient’s consent is obtained before giving the result to a nominated third party.
The service offers relevant health education to its patients in a planned and consistent manner which enables patients to meet their on-going health needs.
Posters and pamphlets regarding conditions relevant to the hospice and palliative care service population are visibly displayed and accessible and routinely provided to patients when appropriate.
There is evidence that family members are taught and supported to provide care for the patient in keeping with the care plan.
Patient and family education provided is noted in the patient record.
The service identifies community resources which support continuing health promotion and disease prevention education and has contact details of these resources available.
There is documented evidence that patients are referred to these resources, where appropriate.
Information is provided in a manner and in a language that is understood by those making the care decisions.
Notes for each consultation contain as minimum points a) – q) above.
Handwritten notes are legible.
Notes are recorded contemporaneously or instantly.
All abbreviations are standardised in accordance with recognised local and national standards.
The patients’ records, including contact details, are up to date to ensure the transfer of the latest information between care providers.
Adverse drug reactions are noted in the patient's record.
The patient is fully informed regarding the estimated costs of any treatment, investigation, procedure or referral and the discussion is documented.
Notice of death forms are completed correctly containing at least details a) – h) above.

Where the death was not exclusively due to natural causes, the death is referred to the police or forensic pathologist and the referral is recorded in the patient record along with the name, position title and contact details of the person to whom the referral has been made.
Copies of notice of death forms completed by the service are filed in the patient records.
Dated entries in the patient record reflect the date the notice of death form was collected and the name of the person to whom the notification was given.
The death notification form is made available for collection within legislated time frames.
There is implementation of a documented policy on clinical handover to ensure that standard processes are followed.
Patients share in decision making regarding the handing over of clinical information.
Handover of clinical information is recorded in the patients' health records.

Information relating to those patients requiring special attention, such as the chronically ill or those with infections which require specific infection control measures, is provided in a patient held letter, a copy of which is kept in the patient record.
There is evidence of one (or a combination) of arrangements for patients to access care outside of normal opening hours.
The credentials of alternative care providers are verified.
Patient health records contain reports or notes of consultations occurring outside normal opening hours by or on behalf of the palliative care service.
The hospice and palliative care service has a documented policy on home and other visits available to regular patients of the service which includes as least a) – d) in the standard intent above.
These visits are available within and outside normal opening hours of the hospice and palliative care service.
Where home visits are not deemed safe or reasonable, patients are provided with information on alternative systems of care.
Written guidelines are available and followed relating to the provision of primary emergency services.
Guidelines for emergency triage, assessment and treatment are available and followed for all types of patients seen at the palliative care service (for example adult, paediatric and neonatal).
Information on cases and the outcome of emergency treatment are recorded in a register/logbook.
Case reviews are undertaken within the service to assess the quality of treatment and care of patients requiring emergency care.
There is a protocol that delineates how the service evaluates, manages, stabilises and transfers patients with emergency conditions.
The service has an implemented resuscitation policy.
All service personnel are trained in basic resuscitation techniques at least every two years, with records of their attendance at such training.
All medical practitioners are updated in medication protocols for resuscitation and resuscitation techniques at least every two years with records of their attendance at such training.

A designated person documents daily checks to ensure that resuscitation medication and equipment is available, in working order and that medication is not expired and reports any adverse findings for immediate remediation.
There is a designated resuscitation area.
There is a mechanism for the summoning of medical help in an emergency.
The service provides resuscitation equipment according to the services provided.
Diagnostic and vital sign monitoring equipment is available as per palliative care service policy.

Equipment for early cardiopulmonary resuscitation is available within one minute in each area of the palliative care service and access to a defibrillator or automated external defibrillator (AED) within three minutes of any patient collapsing.
The service has access to Ambulance Services (EMS).
A designated individual who is suitably qualified has clearly defined responsibilities and accountability for all aspects of medication management.
Medication is prescribed and dispensed in accordance with legislation and current pharmaceutical, medical and nursing guidelines.
The scope of and limitations to the responsibilities and activities of the personnel who manage medication are clearly defined in written policies.
Medication is securely and legibly labelled with relevant information as required by policy.
A register is maintained of all medication dispensed.
The person prescribing and dispensing the medication has access to patient information that would contra-indicate prescription of particular medication.
The person dispensing medication informs the patient of available generic equivalents.
Controlled medication is prescribed and dispensed in accordance with legislation and policy.
Verbal/telephonic medication orders are documented in the patient’s record.
Prescriptions conform to all legal requirements.

Policies and procedures are implemented to ensure that medication is transported to the service and stored according to manufacturers’ guidelines, with specific emphasis on maintenance of cold chain requirements.
A dedicated refrigerator is available for medication requiring storage at low temperatures.
A monitoring log is kept of the temperature within the refrigerator and/or cold-chain monitors and is available for inspection.
The fridge thermometer is calibrated at regular intervals determined by the service and the calibration is documented.
Any remedial action taken is recorded.
The integrity of door seals, door and hinges is checked at regular intervals and documented accordingly.
Medication storage areas are protected from heat and light and effectively ventilated.
Stored medication is legibly marked and securely labelled.
Controlled drugs are stored in a cabinet of substantial construction, for which only authorised personnel have the keys
Controlled drugs are stored in suitable facilities which include but are not limited to: lockable storage facilities, ceiling cages, burglar guards and alarm systems with keypads.
Controlled drugs are accurately accounted for in a specific register which is updated contemporaneously and available for inspection.
Controlled drugs that have expired are disposed of in accordance with legal requirements.
All pharmaceuticals and medical consumables are regularly checked for expiry dates and checks are recorded.
An inventory management system either manual (stock cards) or automated, is in place to monitor maximum and minimum stock levels and control stock losses.
Patient and family rights are identified in a policy and documented in accordance with relevant and current laws, charters and regulations.
Documented evidence of personnel training in relation to these policies and procedures is available.
There is a patient rights charter which is prominently displayed, and patients are made aware of their rights.
Patients are informed about their condition and the proposed care plan.
The service informs patients and their families about their rights to refuse or discontinue the care provided, and the consequences of such decisions.

When patients/carers refuse or discontinue treatment, the consultation regarding their decision is accurately and contemporaneously recorded and includes the details of the discussion regarding the consequences of the decision.
The patient’s need for privacy is protected during all examinations, procedures and treatments.
Patient privacy is protected when providing personal information.
The patient’s right to privacy is protected for all forms of counselling.
Policies and procedures to prevent the loss or misuse of patient information are implemented.
When appropriate, patients are permitted to be accompanied by a family member/care-giver during consultations.
The service has a documented policy outlining the procedure for obtaining general consent for treatment.

The service has a documented policy outlining the procedure for obtaining consent in the case of patients who are unable to grant consent for themselves by way of age or mental/physical incapacity, which is in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.
Documented consent is obtained from patients for health information to be provided to a third party.
Where hospice and palliative care service members use patient information for research, approval has been obtained from the relevant ethics committee and from patients themselves where required.
There is a documented policy outlining the mechanism to allow for the hearing of complaints and how to act upon them which is implemented
Patients are aware of their right to voice complaints and the processes by which to do so, internally as well as externally where applicable.
A nominated individual within the service is responsible for managing the complaints and ensuring that the complaints policy is implemented.
A nominated individual within the service is responsible to oversee the investigation of and response to the complaint.
Complaints are monitored, and repetitions or patterns are identified.
Any opportunities for improvement identified from the investigation of complaints are implemented.
